entries.title: What HTTP Parameter Pollution is
entries.body:
HTTP Parameter Pollution (HPP) is a bypass technique in which an attacker passes several instances of the same parameter with the same name (?param1=value&param1=value) to influence the application logic or extract hidden information.

There is no formal standard for parsing repeated parameters: each technology parses them in its own way. Some take the first occurrence, others the last, others collect everything into an array. It is exactly this discrepancy that gets exploited - for example, to bypass signature-based filters/WAFs, or to split an attack vector across several copies of a parameter.

entries.title: Where to look and the two levels of HPP
entries.body:
HPP hits at two levels:
• Client-Side HPP - exploits the JavaScript code on the client side (in the browser): user input ends up in links/forms, and an extra parameter overrides the frontend behavior.
• Server-Side HPP - exploits how the server handles several parameters with the same name.

Where to try:
• Any endpoint where a single parameter participates in logic (flags, amounts, identifiers, access filters).
• Points that pass through several layers (WAF -> proxy -> backend) - if the layers parse duplicates differently, the filter sees one value while the backend uses another.
• Forms, redirects, links that are assembled from input on the client.

How to test: duplicate the target parameter and observe which value was actually applied; change the order of the values; try different notations (param[], %26, nested keys).

entries.title: Tools
entries.body:
• Burp Suite - edit the request manually and add duplicate parameters.
• OWASP ZAP - intercept and modify HTTP parameters.

entries.title: Encoded injection (gluing on an extra parameter)
JSON injection (duplicate key in the body)
